_about: Image manifest for the Axis tool. Captions display on the back of each card (flip), never the front. No axis position is recorded here on purpose: the tool must not pre-answer the question it asks. File names are placeholders to match to your low-res image files. Shuffle the images array on load so no order is implied.

source.credit: © the respective artists. Shown here at low resolution for study only. To see the work as it should be seen, visit Autograph's gallery in London and explore their collection and exhibitions online.

images.caption: Rotimi Fani-Kayode, 'Nothing to Lose', 1989
images.bio: Rotimi Fani-Kayode (1955-1989) was born in Lagos and came to England as a child after his family fled the Biafran war, later training in the United States before settling in Brixton. A founder of Autograph, he made staged black-and-white studio portraits of the Black male body that drew on Yoruba spirituality and his own life as a gay African in exile. He treated the photograph as a place of desire and ritual rather than record.

images.caption: Joy Gregory, from the series 'Autoportrait', 1989-1990
images.bio: Joy Gregory (b. 1959) is a British artist of Jamaican heritage who trained at Manchester Polytechnic and the Royal College of Art and has long been associated with Autograph. She works across many processes, from self-portraiture to cameraless techniques such as the cyanotype, using the body, hair and everyday objects to examine beauty, race and femininity. The often handmade, antique methods are part of the meaning.

images.caption: Syd Shelton, 'Tulse Hill School, Brixton', 1977
images.bio: Syd Shelton (b. 1947) is an English photographer who trained as a painter in Yorkshire and became one of the defining image-makers of Rock Against Racism in the late 1970s. He photographed Black British life and anti-racist struggle as a committed white participant who stood alongside his subjects rather than visiting them. His pictures belong to the engaged, political documentary tradition.

images.caption: Ingrid Pollard, 'The Valentine Days #2', 2017
images.bio: Ingrid Pollard (b. 1953) was born in Georgetown, Guyana, and works in London, making carefully constructed photographs that question who is felt to belong in the English landscape. She often hand-tints or stages her images and works in series, using portrait and landscape to unsettle ideas of Englishness and rural space. Her position is that of an insider interrogating places that treat her as a stranger.

images.caption: Sunil Gupta, 'Untitled #06', 2008
images.bio: Sunil Gupta (b. 1953) was born in New Delhi, grew up partly in Canada, and is based in London, having learned photography in the documentary tradition in New York. A gay activist as well as an artist, he makes work about queer desire, migration and HIV, moving between street photography and elaborately staged scenes. He photographs communities he belongs to, so his insider position is openly declared.

images.caption: Yinka Shonibare, from the series 'Effnik', 1997
images.bio: Yinka Shonibare (b. 1962) is a British-Nigerian artist who grew up between London and Lagos and trained at Goldsmiths. He is known for using Dutch wax 'African' fabric to unpick the entangled histories of empire and identity, and for staged, theatrical pictures that toy with class, disability and the trappings of Victorian Britain. His images are deliberately constructed and ironic.

images.caption: Anthony Lam, 'Latif's Granddaughter', 1992-93
images.bio: Anthony Lam (b. 1966) was born in Hong Kong and works in Britain on themes of identity, culture and place. He spent years quietly documenting the streets and communities of the East End, especially the Bengali neighbourhoods around Spitalfields and Tower Hamlets. He photographs as a fellow migrant making a record of a place in change.

images.caption: Bandele 'Tex' Ajetunmobi, from the series 'East End Portraits', 1950-1980
images.bio: Bandele 'Tex' Ajetunmobi (1921-1994) was a Nigerian-born photographer who settled in London's East End and was largely self-taught, working with a hand-held camera despite failing eyesight. Over several decades he made warm black-and-white portraits of his neighbours and of the African and Caribbean communities around him. He worked as an insider, picturing the people among whom he lived.

images.caption: Colin Jones, from the series 'The Black House', 1973-76
images.bio: Colin Jones (1936-2021) was an English photographer, a former Royal Ballet dancer who became a photojournalist for the Observer and others. His best-known work, The Black House, documented a hostel for young Black people in north London in the mid-1970s, made over a sustained period of access. He worked as a sympathetic but commissioned outsider, which is part of what the pictures ask you to weigh.

images.caption: Ajamu X, 'Bodybuilder in Bra', 1990
images.bio: Ajamu X (b. 1963), known simply as Ajamu, is a British photographer from Huddersfield who makes bold black-and-white studio portraits of the Black queer body. An archivist and activist as well as an artist, he celebrates desire, intimacy and gender play from firmly inside the communities he photographs. The studio is his chosen ground, so the work is staged and self-possessed.

images.caption: Poulomi Desai, from the series 'Our cultures are the portals – the gateways between one world and the next', 2020
images.bio: Poulomi Desai is a British South Asian artist, musician and curator based in London, with roots in the radical Asian, anti-racist and queer cultures of 1970s and 1980s East London. She works across photography, performance and sound, founded the arts organisation Usurp, and treats the camera as one instrument among many for disturbing how marginalised people are seen. Her images lean towards the experimental and the staged.

images.caption: Aida Silvestri, 'Cacao', from the series 'Contagion: Colour on the Front Line', 2020
images.bio: Aida Silvestri (b. 1978) was born in Eritrea and works in London, having studied photography at Kensington and Chelsea College and the University of Westminster. A self-described visual activist, she makes experimental, mixed-media documentary portraits about migration, identity and health, often working into the surface of the print and adding text. Cacao comes from her Autograph commission on Covid-19 and frontline workers of colour.

images.caption: Reena Saini Kallat, 'United Kingdom', from the series 'Pattern Recognition', 2022
images.bio: Reena Saini Kallat (b. 1973) was born in New Delhi and works in Mumbai across photography, drawing, sculpture and installation. Her Pattern Recognition series turns the official languages of borders, passports and visas into decorative pattern, examining migration and the bureaucracy of belonging. Her approach is conceptual and constructed.

images.caption: Jeannette Ehlers, 'We're Magic. We're Real. #3' (From Sunset to Sunrise) #9, 2021-22
images.bio: Jeannette Ehlers (b. 1973) is a Danish-Trinidadian artist based in Copenhagen who works with photography, video and performance around the legacies of slavery and the colonial Atlantic. Her staged, often spectral images use her own body and ritual to make buried histories visible. The work is performative and symbolic rather than documentary.

images.caption: Henna Nadeem, 'California', 1999-2000
images.bio: Henna Nadeem (b. 1966) is a British artist of Pakistani and English heritage who trained at the Royal College of Art. She is known for cut and collaged pictures that overlay landscape photographs with decorative Islamic and other patterns, interrupting the picturesque with ornament. The work is made by hand on the page rather than out in the field.

images.caption: Jermaine Francis, from the series 'A Pleasant Land - The Muted Sounds of a Black Odyssey', 2024
images.bio: Jermaine Francis is a British photographer based in London, working across street photography, documentary and the personal essay, with a recurring interest in identity, memory and belonging in Britain. A Pleasant Land turns to the English landscape and everyday scenes to reflect on the Black experience and a sense of place. He moves between the caught moment and the quietly composed.

images.caption: Abi Morocco Studios, 'Funmilayo and Jon Abe holding their medium format camera', Aina Street, Shogunle, 1974
images.bio: Abi Morocco Photos was the studio of the husband-and-wife photographers John Abe (1946-2024) and Funmilayo Abe (b. 1953), who worked from Aina Street in the Shogunle district of Lagos from the early 1970s to 2006. Largely self-taught, they made studio portraits, home commissions and street pictures of everyday Lagosians at their most stylish. This is their own self-portrait, so here the makers and the subjects are the same people.

images.caption: Marie Smith, from the series 'The Wanderer', 2021-ongoing
images.bio: Marie Smith is a British visual artist and writer, born and based in London, with an MA in History of Art with Photography from Birkbeck. In The Wanderer they follow a persona of their own making through landscapes, working in film and cyanotype and often leaving the cable release or tripod in shot to admit the camera. Smith is at once the maker and the subject, picturing the Black, non-binary body in nature.

images.caption: James Barnor, 'Untitled #2, two friends dressed for a church celebration', Accra, 1970s
images.bio: James Barnor (b. 1929) is a Ghanaian photographer and a pioneer of his country's image-making, who ran the Ever Young studio in Accra, shot for Drum magazine, and helped bring colour photography to Ghana while also working in London. His pictures move between studio portrait and the street, recording Ghanaian independence and the Black diaspora in Britain with warmth. He photographs his own world from the inside.

images.caption: Ernest Cole, Harlem, New York, c. 1970
images.bio: Ernest Cole (1940-1990) was a South African photographer whose book House of Bondage exposed the daily workings of apartheid and forced him into exile in 1966. In America he turned his camera on Black life in Harlem and the rural South, a Black photographer who was also a stranger far from home. His negatives were lost for decades and only rediscovered after his death.

images.caption: Laura El-Tantawy, from the series 'She Fights in the Fields', 2022
images.bio: Laura El-Tantawy (b. 1980) is an Egyptian photographer raised between Egypt, the United States and Britain, known for lyrical, impressionistic colour work such as her book on Tahrir Square. She works at the edge of documentary, favouring mood, memory and ambiguity over plain reportage. She Fights in the Fields turns to women's labour on the land.

images.caption: Clovis Salmon (aka Sam the Wheels), two film stills from 'Somerleyton 6', a documentary film about the aftermath of the Brixton Riots, 1981
images.bio: Clovis Salmon (b. 1928), known as Sam the Wheels, came to Britain from Jamaica with the Windrush generation and settled in Brixton. Using a hand-held 8mm camera, he filmed his own community from the 1960s onward, including the aftermath of the 1981 Brixton uprising. His footage is the record of an insider filming the street where he lived.

images.caption: Sheida Soleimani, 'Magistrate', from the series 'Flyways', 2024
images.bio: Sheida Soleimani (b. 1990) is an Iranian-American artist, the daughter of political refugees, who teaches in the United States and is also a wildlife rehabilitator. She builds elaborate studio tableaux from collage, props and taxidermy to address human rights, migration and state violence. Her pictures are wholly constructed, so nothing in them is found.

images.caption: Zanele Muholi, 'Ntozakhe II', Parktown, 2016
images.bio: Zanele Muholi (b. 1972) is a South African visual activist who documents and celebrates Black LGBTQIA+ life in South Africa. Their ongoing series Somnyama Ngonyama, to which this self-portrait belongs, deepens the skin tones and builds crowns and props from everyday materials, meeting the viewer's eye head on. The work is staged, political and made from within the community it pictures.

images.caption: Joy Gregory, from the series 'Girl Thing', 2002-05
images.bio: Joy Gregory (b. 1959) is a British artist of Jamaican heritage, trained at Manchester Polytechnic and the Royal College of Art and long associated with Autograph. Much of her work is cameraless, made by laying objects straight onto light-sensitive paper, as in this cyanotype, so the picture is a contact trace rather than a lens image. She uses such methods to think about beauty, femininity and the things women are given to wear.

images.caption: Eileen Perrier, from the series 'Ghana', 1995-1996
images.bio: Eileen Perrier (b. 1974) is a British photographer of Ghanaian and Dominican heritage who trained at the Surrey Institute and the Royal College of Art. She makes calm, frontal studio and location portraits, often in series, drawing on the conventions of the family photograph and the typology. This work was made on a return to Ghana, so she stands between insider and visitor.

images.caption: Andrew Jackson, from the series 'From a Small Island', 2018
images.bio: Andrew Jackson is a British photographer of Jamaican heritage, based in Birmingham, whose work deals with migration, family and memory, and with the Windrush generation's idea of home. From a Small Island was made in Jamaica, looking at the island his parents left, in a loose and personal documentary style. He photographs as a returning son rather than a detached observer.
